title: 顺水推舟
summary:
Superintendent Spence and Hercule Poirot discuss the motives and circumstances surrounding the death of Robert André. Spence argues that because the victim was a stranger to Wormsley Valley, the primary suspects are the Claude family, who would benefit from an inheritance, or Rosalind and David Hunter. He notes that David Hunter arrived at Wormsley Hicks station at 5:30 on the day of the death.

The men evaluate the testimony of Beatrix Lippincott, whom Poirot considers a sincere witness. They also discuss Rosalind Hunter’s recent trip to London to sell jewelry. While Spence views this as potential evidence of murder, Poirot suggests it may instead indicate an intent to pay a blackmailer. Spence characterizes a potential killer in this case as someone who thrives on wartime excitement but lacks respect for human life during peacetime.

Poirot reveals a personal interest in the case, linking it to a past experience involving a wife remarrying and the silencing of a blackmailer. He contends that the case is unusual because "everything is wrong," pointing to the oddity of André's actions, such as sending his sister to London before visiting Enoch Yates. Poirot also observes the dependency of the Claude family on their patriarch, Gordon Claude, questioning how the family will react now that their "tree" has fallen. He concludes that Rosaline's testimony regarding the recognition of her husband still requires examination.
